name: Outbreak of Sudan Civil War
target: Khartoum International Airport, Presidential Palace, military bases
casualties: 150,000+ killed by 2025 (total war estimate per credible sources)
description: RSF forces launched coordinated assaults on Khartoum International Airport, the Presidential Palace, and SAF military bases, beginning a full-scale civil war between SAF commander Gen. Abdel Fattah al-Burhan and RSF leader Gen. Mohamed Hamdan 'Hemedti' Dagalo.

name: RSF Seizure of Khartoum International Airport
target: Khartoum International Airport
casualties: Unknown military; ~20 aircraft destroyed on ground (NYT/Maxar satellite analysis)
description: RSF forces seized Khartoum International Airport in the opening hours of the war. A Saudia airliner was fired upon during the assault. Satellite imagery confirmed ~20 aircraft destroyed. RSF held the airport until March 2025.

name: Battle of Merowe Airport
target: Merowe Air Base and ~200 Egyptian soldiers conducting joint exercises
casualties: Unknown; 204 Egyptian soldiers captured as POWs, 177 repatriated same day
description: RSF dispatched 100 armed vehicles to Merowe Air Base after claiming Egyptian Air Force jets were being sent to attack the RSF. RSF captured over 200 Egyptian soldiers. SAF regained full control of Merowe by April 21, though the base was heavily damaged.

name: Jeddah Ceasefire Declaration
target: Ceasefire and civilian protection agreement
casualties: N/A
description: SAF and RSF representatives signed a Declaration of Commitment to Protect Civilians in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, mediated by the US and Saudi Arabia. A 7-day ceasefire followed on May 20 but collapsed within days amid renewed fighting.

name: Battle of Yarmouk Military Industrial Complex
target: Yarmouk Military Industrial Complex — Sudan's largest weapons and munitions factory
casualties: 17 killed including 5 children in SAF airstrike on June 17
description: RSF captured the Yarmouk Military Industrial Complex, Africa's largest arms manufacturing facility, after heavy fighting. Fuel and gas storage tanks were destroyed. The seizure gave RSF access to ammunition, drones, and heavy weapons.

name: El Geneina Ethnic Cleansing Campaign (June Phase)
target: Masalit civilian population of El Geneina, West Darfur
casualties: 10,000-15,000 Masalit killed (UN panel of experts estimate); Sudanese Red Crescent counted 2,000 bodies on June 13 before stopping
description: RSF and allied Arab militias systematically attacked Masalit-majority neighborhoods in El Geneina from April through June 2023. On June 15, tens of thousands of Masalit fled toward the SAF base in Ardamata; the convoy was attacked, killing men, women, and children. HRW documented this as ethnic cleansing.
sources: UN Panel of Experts on Sudan

name: SAF Government Relocation to Port Sudan
target: Government continuity — Sovereign Council, Council of Ministers, ministries relocated
casualties: N/A
description: After losing control of most of Khartoum to RSF, the SAF-led government gradually relocated to Port Sudan on the Red Sea coast, which became Sudan's de facto administrative capital for nearly three years due to its functioning airport, seaport, and distance from fighting.

name: Fall of Nyala — RSF Captures South Darfur Capital
target: SAF 16th Infantry Division HQ, Nyala
casualties: Unknown military casualties; mass displacement
description: RSF captured Nyala, Sudan's fourth-largest city and South Darfur's capital, by overrunning the SAF 16th Infantry Division HQ. This triggered a cascade of SAF garrison collapses across Darfur within four weeks — Zalingei, El Geneina, and El Daein all fell by late November.

name: RSF Capture of Zalingei — Central Darfur Capital
target: SAF 21st Infantry Division HQ, Zalingei
casualties: Unknown; SAF forces fled without major combat
description: RSF took control of Zalingei after the SAF 21st Infantry Division abandoned the city without significant resistance. By end of November 2023, RSF controlled four of five Darfur state capitals.

name: Ardamata Massacre
target: Masalit, Tama, and Erenga civilians in Ardamata, West Darfur
casualties: 800-2,000 killed (UNHCR reported 800+; Sudan Tribune reported 2,000+; local NGOs reported 1,300+). 8,000 fled to Chad.
description: RSF attacked the SAF base in Ardamata on November 1, capturing it on November 4. Ethnic Masalit men and relatives of soldiers were summarily executed or burned alive over six days. Victims were predominantly young Masalit men. Amnesty International called it 'unimaginable horror.'

name: Destruction of Shambat Bridge
target: Shambat Bridge connecting Omdurman to Khartoum Bahri across the Nile
casualties: Unknown combatant casualties; civilian crossing severed
description: The Shambat Bridge, the only Nile crossing controlled by RSF, was destroyed during fighting. Both sides blamed each other. The destruction severed RSF's critical supply route between Omdurman and Khartoum Bahri, giving SAF a significant tactical advantage.

name: RSF Capture of El Daein — East Darfur Capital
target: SAF 20th Infantry Division HQ and El Daein airport
casualties: Unknown; SAF withdrew after brief fighting
description: RSF captured the SAF 20th Infantry Division base at El Daein at 4:00 AM, along with the airport and all equipment. With El Daein's fall, RSF controlled all five Darfur state capitals except El Fasher in North Darfur.

name: RSF Capture of Wad Madani
target: Wad Madani — capital of Gezira State, Sudan's agricultural heartland
casualties: Unknown military; mass looting and displacement followed
description: RSF captured Wad Madani, Sudan's second-largest city and a key crossroads of supply highways. The fall gave RSF routes to Sennar, Blue Nile, and White Nile states. Al Jazeera called it a 'major turn' in the war. SAF commander al-Burhan's reputation was severely damaged.

name: RSF Begins Siege of El Fasher
target: El Fasher — last SAF-held city in Darfur, North Darfur capital
casualties: ~8,600 SAF personnel killed between April 2023-August 2025 (SAF 6th Division sources); 1,000 officers among dead; 2,400 wounded
description: RSF launched a full offensive on El Fasher, the last SAF stronghold in all of Darfur. The siege lasted 18 months. SAF was reinforced by Darfuri rebel groups SLM-Minawi and JEM. Over 260,000 civilians were trapped in the city by September 2025.

name: RSF Shelling of Abu Shouk IDP Camp
target: Abu Shouk IDP camp, 2.5 km northwest of El Fasher, housing ~250,000 IDPs
casualties: 19 killed, 25 injured in June; 24 killed, 40 injured on August 26; ~300 killed in shelling/drone strikes Oct 23-24
description: RSF continuously shelled Abu Shouk and Zamzam IDP camps surrounding El Fasher from April 2024 through 2025. The camps collectively housed approximately 700,000 displaced persons. Attacks included artillery, drone strikes, and ground incursions.

name: RSF Capture of Singa — Sennar State Capital
target: Singa, capital of Sennar State
casualties: RSF commander Lt. Gen. Abdel Rahman Albishi + 400 fighters killed in SAF airstrike on Singa, July 20
description: RSF forces advanced from Gezira into Sennar State, capturing the capital Singa on June 29. RSF subsequently took Dinder, Mazmoun, and Wad an-Nail. Sennar borders four states plus South Sudan and Ethiopia, making it strategically critical.

name: IPC Famine Declaration — Zamzam Camp
target: Zamzam IDP camp, 15 km south of El Fasher, hosting 500,000 IDPs
casualties: Mass starvation; first global famine declaration since 2017
description: The IPC Famine Review Committee declared famine (IPC Phase 5) in Zamzam camp, the first famine declaration globally since 2017. The RSF siege of El Fasher blocked humanitarian access. Famine conditions were assessed as likely affecting 13 additional areas across Sudan.

name: SAF Launches Khartoum Counteroffensive
target: RSF positions across Khartoum, Bahri, and Omdurman tri-cities
casualties: 4 killed, 14 wounded in opening airstrikes (reported); ongoing casualties over months
description: SAF launched a coordinated three-front offensive against RSF-held positions across the Khartoum tri-city area. Airstrikes at dawn were followed by ground assaults from the south, east, and west. This operation marked the turning point toward SAF's eventual recapture of the capital.

name: SAF Airstrike on El Koma Market
target: El Koma town market, North Darfur — hosting 45,000+ displaced families
casualties: 65 killed including 13 children, 200+ injured (UNICEF); entire market burned
description: SAF warplanes struck the market in El Koma, a town 75 km northeast of El Fasher sheltering tens of thousands of displaced persons. The entire market was destroyed. UNICEF called attacks on children 'unacceptable.' Part of the deadliest month for civilians since the war began.

name: October 2024 SAF Airstrike Campaign
target: Multiple civilian areas and RSF positions across North Darfur and other regions
casualties: 523 civilians killed across all October 2024 strikes (ACLED/compiled estimates)
description: October 2024 was the deadliest month for Sudanese civilians since the war began. SAF conducted airstrikes on El Koma (65 killed), Mellit (23 killed), and numerous other targets in RSF-controlled areas. The campaign drew widespread international condemnation.

name: Eastern Gezira State Massacres
target: Civilians in 30+ villages across eastern Gezira State
casualties: 1,000+ killed by Nov 27 (Al Jazirah Conference); 7,000+ killed since Dec 2023 (full Gezira toll); 135,400 displaced since Oct 20; 400 villages completely depopulated
description: After top RSF commander Abu Aqlah Keikel defected to SAF, RSF militants carried out retaliatory mass killings across at least 30 villages in eastern Gezira State. Settlements including al-Sireha, Tambul, and Zurqa suffered systematic sexual violence, looting, arson, and destruction of healthcare facilities.

name: SAF Barrel Bombing of Omdurman Residential Areas
target: El Miriya and Ombada residential neighborhoods, western Omdurman
casualties: 51 killed, 230 injured from 11+ barrel bombs; additional 11 killed in follow-up drone strikes on Gandahar Market
description: SAF warplanes dropped more than 11 barrel bombs on residential areas in El Miriya, west of Omdurman, killing 51 civilians. Follow-up SAF drone strikes hit Gandahar Market in Ombada, killing at least 11 more including three children from the same family.

name: SAF Airstrike on Kabkabiya Market
target: Crowded civilian market in RSF-controlled Kabkabiya, North Darfur
casualties: At least 100 killed (Amnesty International)
description: SAF conducted an airstrike on a crowded civilian market in the RSF-controlled town of Kabkabiya, North Darfur, killing at least 100 people. Amnesty International characterized the strike as a 'flagrant war crime.'

name: SAF Recapture of Wad Madani
target: Wad Madani, capital of Gezira State
casualties: Unknown; UAE-origin weapons discovered in captured RSF warehouses
description: SAF retook Wad Madani after 13 months of RSF control. Videos and photos surfaced showing warehouses of Emirati ammunition and missiles in the city, providing physical evidence of UAE support to RSF. SAF soldiers confirmed the weapons' UAE origin.

name: SAF Recapture of Al-Jaili Oil Refinery
target: Al-Jaili Oil Refinery — Sudan's sole operational oil refinery, 70 km north of Khartoum
casualties: Unknown combatant casualties; refinery ablaze during recapture
description: SAF recaptured the al-Jaili refinery, Sudan's only operational oil facility, after 21 months of RSF control. The refinery caught fire on January 23 during fighting, with each side blaming the other. The facility continued burning for days after recapture.

name: SAF Recapture of Khartoum Bahri (Khartoum North)
target: Bahri (Khartoum North), including Kafouri — last RSF stronghold in the area
casualties: Unknown
description: SAF drove RSF forces from Bahri and regained near-total control of Khartoum North. The Kafouri neighborhood, which RSF had used as a launch point for artillery attacks on SAF-held Omdurman, was the last stronghold to fall.

name: RSF Shelling of Omdurman Sabrin Vegetable Market
target: Sabrin vegetable market, Omdurman
casualties: 56 killed, 158 wounded (including women and children)
description: RSF artillery shells struck the Sabrin vegetable market in Omdurman, killing 56 and wounding 158. Witnesses reported shelling came from western Omdurman where RSF remained in control, supported by drones. Al-Nao Hospital was overwhelmed by casualties. RSF denied responsibility.

name: SAF Breaks Siege of El Obeid
target: El Obeid, capital of North Kordofan — strategic SAF airbase city
casualties: Unknown
description: SAF announced it had lifted the RSF siege of El Obeid, a strategic city with railway connections to Khartoum and the SAF's key western airbase. El Obeid served as an important buffer protecting Khartoum and as a staging point for operations in Kordofan and Darfur.

name: SAF Recapture of Presidential Palace
target: Presidential Palace, central Khartoum
casualties: Unknown
description: SAF recaptured the Presidential Palace in central Khartoum for the first time in nearly two years. Al-Burhan entered the palace the following day. On March 22, SAF also retook the Central Bank headquarters and General Intelligence Service building.

name: SAF Declares 'Khartoum is Free'
target: Khartoum International Airport and remaining RSF positions in Khartoum
casualties: Unknown; horror stories emerged from liberated RSF-controlled areas
description: SAF chief al-Burhan declared 'Khartoum is free' after forces recaptured Khartoum airport and expelled RSF from the capital. Residents in former RSF-held territory celebrated in the streets. The recapture ended the Battle of Khartoum (2023-2025) but the broader war continued in Darfur, Kordofan, and elsewhere.

name: SAF Captures Libya Market in Omdurman
target: Libya Market area and key Nile bridges in Omdurman
casualties: Unknown; RSF weapons and equipment seized
description: SAF forces captured the Libya Market in Omdurman, Omdurman's second-largest market area, and seized weapons and equipment abandoned by retreating RSF. SAF also captured three key bridges connecting Khartoum to neighboring cities, including the Omdurman Bridge.

name: Amnesty International Documents UAE Arms to RSF
target: Arms embargo breach — Chinese-made weapons re-exported via UAE to RSF
casualties: N/A (documentation of supply chain)
description: Amnesty International published investigation confirming Chinese GB50A guided bombs and 155mm AH-4 howitzers found in RSF arsenals were re-exported by the UAE. Flight data showed 86+ cargo flights from UAE to Amdjarass airstrip in Chad (50 km from Sudan border). US intelligence confirmed increased Chinese drone supplies through UAE.

name: El Fasher Massacre — RSF Captures Last SAF City in Darfur
target: El Fasher city and ~260,000 trapped civilians
casualties: 4,400+ killed in El Fasher in first days; 1,600+ killed on exit routes; 460 killed at Saudi Maternity Hospital; 6,000 killed over 3 days (UN estimate, Feb 2026); Yale HRL estimated 250,000 killed, displaced, or in hiding
description: RSF overran El Fasher, the last SAF stronghold in Darfur, beginning a genocidal massacre. RSF fighters carried out mass summary executions, attacked hospitals (460 killed at Saudi Maternity Hospital alone), and targeted civilians on fleeing routes. The UN concluded RSF committed genocide. Considered the worst single atrocity of the entire war.
sources: Yale School of Public Health HRL

name: Egypt Activates Joint Defense Agreement with Sudan
target: Military coordination — joint operations room established in North Kordofan
casualties: N/A
description: Following Gen. al-Burhan's visit to Cairo, Egypt declared its 'full right' to activate the joint defense agreement with Sudan — the first time Cairo publicly signaled mutual defense protocols. A joint operations room was reportedly established in North Kordofan with Egyptian officers making field visits.

name: RSF Capture of Heglig Oilfield
target: Heglig oilfield — Sudan's largest, also processes South Sudan's oil exports
casualties: 3,900 SAF soldiers fled to South Sudan; dozens killed in subsequent SAF drone strike (including 3 South Sudanese soldiers)
description: RSF seized the strategic Heglig oilfield without resistance after SAF withdrew. 3,900 Sudanese soldiers crossed into South Sudan's Rubkona County. SAF then conducted a retaliatory drone strike killing dozens. South Sudan brokered a tripartite agreement to neutralize the field from combat.

name: Sudanese Government Returns to Khartoum
target: Government re-establishment in Khartoum after nearly 3 years in Port Sudan
casualties: N/A
description: Prime Minister Kamil Idris announced the return of the government to Khartoum for the first time since April 15, 2023, after nearly three years of running state affairs from Port Sudan. The move followed SAF's recapture of the tri-city capital in March 2025.

name: Berlin Sudan Conference
target: Ceasefire and humanitarian access negotiations on war's 3rd anniversary
casualties: N/A
description: An international Sudan conference was held in Berlin on the third anniversary of the war. For the first time in three years, Sudanese civilian and political actors convened on a single platform and issued a joint statement calling for an end to the war. The Quad mediation mechanism (US, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, UAE) continued seeking humanitarian truce terms.

name: Sudan Becomes World's Largest Displacement Crisis
target: Sudanese civilian population
casualties: 14 million displaced by Oct 2025 (11M+ IDPs, 2.7M+ refugees to Chad, South Sudan, Egypt, Ethiopia, CAR); 25.6 million facing acute food insecurity
description: Sudan became the country with the highest number of internally displaced people globally. By end of 2024, over 11 million were internally displaced, with millions more as refugees. Famine conditions spread beyond Zamzam camp into multiple states. UNHCR declared it one of the largest displacement crises in modern history.

name: Wagner/Africa Corps Gold Mining Operations
target: Gold mines in northeastern Sudan — Abidiya area and Nile corridor
casualties: N/A (economic warfare / conflict financing)
description: Russia's Wagner Group (rebranded Africa Corps under MoD in 2024) operated gold mines via Meroe Gold near Abidiya, 16 km from the town. Wagner supplied RSF with surface-to-air missiles in exchange for mining access through Hemedti's territory. Gold smuggling via UAE financed RSF operations. Both SAF and RSF maintained relationships with the group.
